description: Holmes and Watson collaborate as consulting detectives and share a residence, with Watson chronicling their cases.
context: I am bound to say, occasionally to embellish, you have given prominence not so much to the many causes c´ el` ebres and sensational trials in which I have figured but rather to those incidents which may have been trivial in themselves, but which have given room for those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.

description: Holmes critiques Watson's literary style, arguing it prioritizes sensationalism over strict logical reasoning.

properties.quote: You have erred perhaps in attempting to put colour and life into each of your statements instead of confining yourself to the task of placing upon record that severe reasoning from cause to effect

context: You have erred perhaps in attempting to put colour and life into each of your statements instead of confining yourself to the task of placing upon record that severe reasoning from cause to effect which is really the only notable feature about the thing.

description: Holmes meets Violet Hunter as a new client seeking professional advice on an employment offer.
context: I could see that Holmes was favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client.

description: Holmes offers to consult with Violet Hunter regarding her dilemma about accepting a governess position.
context: I shall be happy to do anything that I can to serve you.

description: Holmes forms a positive impression of Violet Hunter's character, manner, and speech upon meeting her.

properties.quote: favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client

context: I could see that Holmes was favourably impressed by the manner and speech of his new client.

description: Holmes previously helped the King of Bohemia with a delicate matter that fell outside legal boundaries.
context: The small matter in which I endeavoured to help the King of Bohemia...

description: Holmes investigated the singular and unusual experience involving Miss Mary Sutherland.
context: the singular experience of Miss Mary Sutherland...

description: Holmes observes and prepares to interrogate Violet Hunter upon her arrival at his residence.

properties.contribution: greets her and prepares to listen to her story

context: As he spoke the door opened and a young lady entered the room.

description: Violet Hunter arrives at Baker Street to seek Holmes's professional advice.

properties.contribution: enters the room to seek advice

context: As he spoke the door opened and a young lady entered the room.

description: Holmes leads the discussion, arguing that logic should be prioritized over sensationalism in case records.
context: 'At the same time,' he remarked after a pause... 'you can hardly be open to a charge of sensationalism...'

description: Watson defends his writing style against accusations of sensationalism while acknowledging the charge.

properties.contribution: defends his records and acknowledges the charge

context: 'And yet,' said I, smiling, 'I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.'

description: Violet Hunter visits Baker Street to consult Holmes regarding her employment dilemma.
context: I shall call at half-past ten to-morrow if I do not inconvenience you.

description: Holmes lives and works in Baker Street, where he conducts his investigations and receives clients.
context: we sat after breakfast on either side of a cheery fire in the old room at Baker Street.

description: Watson lives and works alongside Holmes in Baker Street, sharing the residence.
context: we sat after breakfast on either side of a cheery fire in the old room at Baker Street.

description: Violet Hunter resides at Montague Place, as indicated by the address on her letter to Holmes.
context: It was dated from Montague Place upon the preceding evening, and ran thus:

description: Colonel Munro received an appointment in Halifax, Nova Scotia, and relocated there with his children.
context: the colonel received an appointment at Halifax, in Nova Scotia, and took his children over to America with him

description: Violet Hunter frequently visits the West End to check for governess positions at Westaway's agency.
context: There is a well-known agency for governesses in the West End called Westaway's, and there I used to call about once a week

description: Violet Hunter regularly visits Westaway's agency to seek employment as a governess.
context: There is a well-known agency for governesses in the West End called Westaway's, and there I used to call about once a week

description: Miss Stoper manages Westaway's agency, handling client consultations and employment ledgers.
context: Westaway was the name of the founder of the business, but it is really managed by Miss Stoper.

description: Holmes reads the Daily Telegraph to scan advertisement columns for potential cases.
context: tossing aside the advertisement sheet of the Daily Telegraph

description: Holmes smokes a long cherry-wood pipe during disputatious or argumentative moods.
context: lighting with it the long cherry-wood pipe which was wont to replace his clay when he was in a disputatious rather than a meditative mood

description: Holmes receives and examines a crumpled letter from Violet Hunter regarding her employment dilemma.
context: He tossed a crumpled letter across to me.

description: Holmes previously investigated the Blue Carbuncle, which evolved from a trivial whim into a serious case.
context: You remember that the affair of the blue carbuncle, which appeared to be a mere whim at first, developed into a serious investigation.

description: Holmes gives advice to young ladies from boarding-schools, representing their interests in his declining practice.
context: As to my own little practice, it seems to be degenerating into an agency for recovering lost lead pencils and giving advice to young ladies from boarding-schools.

description: Holmes considers logic and deduction his special province and the core of his professional art.
context: those faculties of deduction and of logical synthesis which I have made my special province.

description: Holmes loves art for its own sake, finding pleasure in its least important and lowliest manifestations.
context: to the man who loves art for its own sake,' remarked Sherlock Holmes

description: Watson's case records are accused of expressing sensationalism rather than strict logical reasoning.
context: I cannot quite hold myself absolved from the charge of sensationalism which has been urged against my records.

description: Holmes actively avoids sensationalism in his cases, preferring logical deduction over trivial or sensational outcomes.
context: But in avoiding the sensational, I fear that you may have bordered on the trivial.

description: Holmes considers his current state of work to be at a 'zero-point', marking a perceived low in his career.
context: This note I had this morning marks my zero-point, I fancy.

Miss Hunter previously worked as a governess for Colonel Spence Munro at a salary of £4 a month.
context: I had £ 4 a month in my last place with Colonel Spence Munro.

description: Miss Hunter attended the interview to apply for a governess position.
context: You are looking for a situation, miss? he asked. As governess? Yes, sir.

description: Miss Hunter negotiated her salary during the interview, initially asking for £4 a month.
context: And what salary do you ask? I had £ 4 a month in my last place with Colonel Spence Munro.

description: Jephro Rucastle offered Miss Hunter a governess position with a salary of £100 a year.
context: Your salary with me, madam, would commence at £ 100 a year.

description: Jephro Rucastle is familiar with Miss Stoper, the agency manageress, and interacts with her during the interview.
context: The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us, but she glanced at me now with so much annoyance upon her face that I could not help suspecting that she had lost a handsome commission through my refusal.

description: Jephro Rucastle is married to Mrs. Rucastle, who dictates the household's eccentric fads.
context: It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted.

description: Jephro Rucastle is the father of Alice, who is currently residing in Philadelphia.
context: ...belonging to my dear daughter Alice (now in Philadelphia)...

description: Jephro Rucastle lives at The Copper Beeches, a country house in Hampshire.
context: Hampshire. Charming rural place. The Copper Beeches, five miles on the far side of Winchester. It is the most lovely country, my dear young lady, and the dearest old country-house.

description: Jephro Rucastle plans to meet Miss Hunter at Winchester with a dog-cart.
context: Now do try to come, and I shall meet you with the dog-cart at Winchester.

description: Miss Stoper works as the manageress of the employment agency.
context: The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us...

description: Miss Stoper evaluates Miss Hunter's suitability for the position and expresses annoyance at her refusal.
context: The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us, but she glanced at me now with so much annoyance upon her face that I could not help suspecting that she had lost a handsome commission through my refusal.

description: Mrs. Rucastle resides at The Copper Beeches and dictates the household's eccentric rules.

properties.note: Wishes for specific dress and hair cutting

context: It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted.

description: Alice currently lives in Philadelphia, away from the family home.
context: ...belonging to my dear daughter Alice (now in Philadelphia)...

description: Miss Hunter receives and possesses the letter from Jephro Rucastle offering increased salary.

properties.content: job offer with increased salary and fad details

context: I have it here and I will read it to you: 'The Copper Beeches, near Winchester. Dear Miss Hunter...'

description: The electric blue dress belongs to Alice and is provided for Miss Hunter to wear.
context: ...belonging to my dear daughter Alice...

description: Miss Hunter is requested to wear the electric blue dress indoors in the morning.
context: My wife is fond of a particular shade of electric blue and would like you to wear such a dress indoors in the morning.

description: Jephro Rucastle owns a pocketbook from which he produces a note during the interview.
context: ...opened a pocketbook and took out a note.

description: The demand to cut her hair triggers Miss Hunter's firm refusal and suspicion.
context: Or to cut your hair quite short before you come to us? I could hardly believe my ears... I am afraid that that is quite impossible, said I.

description: Jephro Rucastle is involved in and promotes the household's strange fads, describing them as eccentric but kind-hearted.
context: We are faddy people, you know-faddy but kind-hearted.

description: The hair-cutting demand is expressed through Jephro Rucastle as a non-negotiable fancy of his wife.
context: It is a little fancy of my wife's, and ladies' fancies, you know, madam, ladies' fancies must be consulted.

description: Jephro Rucastle uses the employment agency to find a governess and maintains contact through Miss Stoper.
context: Miss Stoper has very kindly given me your address, and I write from here to ask you whether you have reconsidered your decision.

description: Miss Hunter is offered a position to reside at The Copper Beeches as a governess.
context: Hampshire. Charming rural place. The Copper Beeches, five miles on the far side of Winchester.

description: Miss Stoper observes the interview and manages the agency's paperwork during the event.
context: The manageress had sat all this while busy with her papers without a word to either of us...

description: Jephro Rucastle conducts the interview to assess Miss Hunter's suitability for the governess role.
context: You are looking for a situation, miss? he asked. As governess? Yes, sir.

description: Miss Hunter participates in the salary negotiation, initially stating her previous wage.

properties.contribution: Stated previous salary and accepted offer

context: And what salary do you ask? I had £ 4 a month in my last place with Colonel Spence Munro.

description: Jephro Rucastle leads the salary negotiation, offering £100 a year and advancing half upfront.
context: Your salary with me, madam, would commence at £ 100 a year. It is also my custom... to advance to my young ladies half their salary beforehand...

Holmes knows Miss Hunter and evaluates her dilemma regarding the job offer.
context: I thought, however, that before taking the final step I should like to submit the whole matter to your consideration.

description: Miss Hunter confides her personal and professional dilemma to Holmes.
context: I thought, however, that before taking the final step I should like to submit the whole matter to your consideration.

description: Holmes evaluates Mr. Rucastle's character and the suspicious nature of the job offer.

properties.quote: not the situation which I should like to see a sister of mine apply for

context: I confess that it is not the situation which I should like to see a sister of mine apply for.

description: Holmes speculates that Mr. Rucastle's wife may be a lunatic kept quiet to avoid an asylum.

properties.quote: Perhaps you have yourself formed some opinion?

context: Is it not possible that his wife is a lunatic, that he desires to keep the matter quiet for fear she should be taken to an asylum...

description: Mr. Rucastle is married to his wife, who is suspected of being mentally unstable.
context: Is it not possible that his wife is a lunatic, that he desires to keep the matter quiet...

description: Mr. Rucastle employs Miss Hunter for a position offering an unusually high salary.
context: Why should they give you £ 120 a year, when they could have their pick for £ 40 ?

description: Holmes works with Dr. Watson as his trusted companion and assistant.
context: Will you come with me?" / "I should wish to.

description: Miss Hunter discovers and receives the letter containing the job offer.
context: That is the letter which I have just received, Mr. Holmes, and my mind is made up that I will accept it.

description: Holmes discovers and receives the urgent telegram summoning him to Winchester.
context: The telegram which we eventually received came late one night just as I was thinking of turning in...

description: Holmes uses Bradshaw to look up train schedules for the journey.
context: Just look up the trains in Bradshaw,

description: Watson uses Bradshaw to check train times and confirm the schedule.
context: There is a train at half-past nine,

description: Miss Hunter visits Winchester to take up the position and meet Holmes.
context: I shall start for Winchester to-morrow.

description: Holmes visits Winchester to investigate Miss Hunter's case and meet her.
context: Please be at the Black Swan Hotel at Winchester at midday to-morrow...

description: Miss Hunter visits Hampshire to travel to her new employer's location.
context: I shall go down to Hampshire quite easy in my mind now.

description: Holmes visits Hampshire to observe the countryside and assess its isolation.
context: after we had passed the Hampshire border he threw them down and began to admire the scenery.

description: Holmes visits the Black Swan Hotel to meet Miss Hunter for the investigation.
context: Please be at the Black Swan Hotel at Winchester at midday to-morrow...

description: Miss Hunter visits the Black Swan Hotel to seek Holmes's help.
context: Please be at the Black Swan Hotel at Winchester at midday to-morrow...

description: Holmes surveils the rolling hills and farmsteadings around Aldershot.
context: All over the countryside, away to the rolling hills around Aldershot, the little red and grey roofs of the farm-steadings peeped out...

description: Holmes resides in London, specifically near Baker Street.
context: fresh from the fogs of Baker Street.

description: Watson resides in London alongside Holmes.
context: fresh from the fogs of Baker Street.

description: Holmes participates in The Job Offer as an investigator evaluating the suspicious circumstances.

properties.contribution: evaluates the situation and advises Miss Hunter

context: I confess that it is not the situation which I should like to see a sister of mine apply for.

description: Miss Hunter participates in The Job Offer as an applicant accepting the position.

properties.contribution: accepts the offer and travels to Hampshire

context: That is the letter which I have just received... I will accept it.

description: Mr. Rucastle participates in The Job Offer as the employer offering the position.
context: Why should they give you £ 120 a year, when they could have their pick for £ 40 ?

description: Holmes participates in The Journey to Winchester as an investigator traveling to assist Miss Hunter.

properties.contribution: travels to Winchester to investigate the case

context: By eleven o'clock the next day we were well upon our way to the old English capital.

description: Miss Hunter participates in The Journey to Winchester as a traveler seeking help.

properties.contribution: goes to Winchester to meet Holmes

context: I shall start for Winchester to-morrow.

description: Watson participates in The Journey to Winchester as a companion assisting Holmes.
context: Will you come with me?" / "I should wish to.

description: Holmes participates in The Chemical Research as a researcher conducting late-night experiments.

properties.contribution: conducts chemical analysis late into the night

context: Holmes was settling down to one of those all-night chemical researches which he frequently indulged in...

description: The Journey to Winchester occurs at Winchester as the destination.
context: Please be at the Black Swan Hotel at Winchester at midday to-morrow...

description: The Journey to Winchester occurs in Hampshire as the region traversed.
context: I shall go down to Hampshire quite easy in my mind now.

description: The Journey to Winchester occurs at the Black Swan Hotel as the meeting point.
context: Please be at the Black Swan Hotel at Winchester at midday to-morrow...

description: Holmes involves Isolation as a dangerous factor that enables crime in the countryside.

properties.quote: feeling of their isolation and of the impunity with which crime may be committed there

context: I look at them, and the only thought which comes to me is a feeling of their isolation...

description: Holmes involves Public Opinion and Law as contrasting forces that maintain justice.

properties.quote: The pressure of public opinion can do in the town what the law cannot accomplish.

context: The pressure of public opinion can do in the town what the law cannot accomplish.

description: Holmes involves Data as the missing element required to solve the case.

properties.quote: Data! data! data! I can't make bricks without clay.

context: Data! data! data!" he cried impatiently. "I can't make bricks without clay.

description: Isolation triggers the need for Public Opinion and Law to function effectively to prevent crime.
context: The pressure of public opinion can do in the town what the law cannot accomplish.

description: Data triggers the investigation of The Job Offer as Holmes seeks more information.
context: I have no data. I cannot tell.

Miss Hunter is the primary subject of the daily ritual performance, following strict instructions to change dress and sit in the window.
context: Two days later this same performance was gone through under exactly similar circumstances. Again I changed my dress, again I sat in the window, and again I laughed very heartily at the funny stories of which my employer had an immense repertoire, and which he told inimitably.

description: Jephro Rucastle is Miss Hunter's employer, dictating her daily routine and reading preferences.
context: the funny stories of which my employer had an immense repertoire, and which he told inimitably.

description: Mrs. Rucastle alerts Jephro to the presence of the man in the road staring at Miss Hunter.
context: 'Jephro,' said she, 'there is an impertinent fellow upon the road there who stares up at Miss Hunter.'

description: Mrs. Rucastle suspects Miss Hunter of hiding a mirror and observing what is behind her.
context: I am convinced that she had divined that I had a mirror in my hand and had seen what was behind me.

description: Mrs. Rucastle is aware of the man's presence and his intent to stare at the window.
context: there is an impertinent fellow upon the road there who stares up at Miss Hunter.

description: Old Toller works as the groom for Jephro Rucastle.
context: old Toller, my groom, is the only man who can do anything with him.

description: Old Toller is the only person capable of handling or controlling the mastiff, Carlo.
context: old Toller, my groom, is the only man who can do anything with him.

description: The man in the road is observed staring earnestly in Miss Hunter's direction from the road.
context: a small bearded man in a grey suit, who seemed to be looking in my direction.

description: The child is Miss Hunter's charge, indicating a supervisory or guardian relationship.
context: I went out for a walk in the grounds with my charge

description: Miss Hunter lives at the Copper Beeches estate, where the strange events take place.
context: On the very first day that I was at the Copper Beeches

description: Miss Hunter is required to wear the blue dress as part of the daily performance.
context: nor have I worn the blue dress

description: Jephro Rucastle claims ownership of the mastiff, Carlo, though he admits Toller handles him.
context: It's only Carlo, my mastiff.

description: Miss Hunter discovers a coil of hair identical to her own locked inside a drawer.
context: It was my coil of hair.

description: Miss Hunter hides her own coil of hair at the bottom of her trunk.
context: I had placed it in a great coil at the bottom of my trunk.

description: Miss Hunter uses the trunk to hide her coil of hair.
context: I had placed it in a great coil at the bottom of my trunk.

description: Miss Hunter returns the strange hair to the locked drawer of the chest of drawers.
context: I returned the strange hair to the drawer

description: Miss Hunter uses a broken piece of her hand-mirror to secretly observe behind her.
context: My hand-mirror had been broken, so a happy thought seized me, and I concealed a piece of the glass in my handkerchief.

description: Jephro Rucastle uses the yellow-backed novel to direct Miss Hunter's reading and control her shadow.
context: Then he handed me a yellow-backed novel, and moving my chair a little sideways, that my own shadow might not fall upon the page, he begged me to read aloud to him.

description: Jephro Rucastle maintains strict control over the locked wing, locking it and showing anger when discovered.
context: He locked the door and hurried past me without a word or a look.

description: Miss Hunter investigates the locked wing by strolling around the grounds to observe its windows.
context: This aroused my curiosity, so when I went out for a walk in the grounds with my charge, I strolled round to the side from which I could see the windows of this part of the house.

description: The man in the road is located on the Southampton Road, leaning against the railings.
context: a man standing in the Southampton Road

description: The daily performance ritual takes place with Miss Hunter sitting in the window.
context: again I sat in the window

description: The mystery of the hair directly involves Miss Hunter, who is puzzled by the identical coil found in the drawer.
context: Was it not extraordinary? Puzzle as I would, I could make nothing at all of what it meant.

description: The coil of hair is the central physical object around which the mystery revolves.
context: It was my coil of hair.

description: Jephro Rucastle is the primary figure associated with the locked wing, guarding it and reacting with anger when discovered.
context: He locked the door and hurried past me without a word or a look.

description: The locked wing itself is the focal point of the mystery, appearing uninhabited but strictly controlled.
context: There was one wing, however, which appeared not to be inhabited at all. A door which faced that which led into the quarters of the Tollers opened into this suite, but it was invariably locked.

Miss Hunter knows Mr. Holmes and seeks his professional advice regarding her situation at Copper Beeches.
context: I thought of you, Mr. Holmes. I could not live there longer without some advice.

description: Miss Hunter feels intense fear and dread towards Mr. Rucastle due to his oppressive and threatening behavior.
context: I was frightened of the house, of the man, of the woman, of the servants, even of the child. They were all horrible to me.

description: Miss Hunter uses the key to unlock the door to the barred room and enter the passage.
context: I turned the key gently in the lock, opened the door, and slipped through.

description: Miss Hunter discovers the iron bed bar and cord used to barricade the door to the dark room.
context: across the outside of it had been fastened one of the broad bars of an iron bed, padlocked at one end to a ring in the wall, and fastened at the other with stout cord.

description: Miss Hunter observes Toller carrying the black linen bag through the forbidden door, sparking her suspicion.
context: I once saw him carrying a large black linen bag with him through the door.

description: Miss Hunter actively investigates the forbidden room and its barred door to uncover the hidden secret.
context: from the moment that I understood that there was something about that suite of rooms which I was not to know, I was all on fire to go over them.

description: Miss Hunter is the target of Mr. Rucastle's threat to throw her to the mastiff if she enters the forbidden room again.
context: I'll throw you to the mastiff.

description: Miss Hunter agrees to assist in Mr. Holmes's plan by locking Mrs. Toller in the wine-cellar.
context: If you could send her into the cellar on some errand, and then turn the key upon her, you would facilitate matters immensely. 'I will do it.'

description: Miss Hunter is responsible for looking after the child while the Rucastles are away.
context: so that I must look after the child.

description: Miss Hunter travels to Winchester to seek Mr. Holmes's advice and assistance.
context: I had no difficulty in getting leave to come into Winchester this morning

description: Miss Hunter attributes her strong urge to investigate the forbidden rooms to woman's instinct.
context: They talk of woman's instinct; perhaps it was woman's instinct which gave me that feeling.

description: Miss Hunter experiences overwhelming terror and a sense of oppression from the house, its inhabitants, and the barred room.
context: A mad, unreasoning terror rose up in me at the sight... I was frightened of the house, of the man, of the woman, of the servants, even of the child. They were all horrible to me.

description: Mr. Holmes investigates the mystery of the barred room and the Rucastle household based on Miss Hunter's account.
context: Holmes and I had listened spellbound to this extraordinary story. My friend rose now and paced up and down the room, his hands in his pockets, and an expression of the most profound gravity upon his face.

description: Mr. Holmes devises and leads the plan to infiltrate the Copper Beeches and uncover the secret.
context: We shall be at the Copper Beeches by seven o'clock, my friend and I. The Rucastles will be gone by that time, and Toller will, we hope, be incapable.

description: Mr. Rucastle deceives Miss Hunter about the purpose of the dark room and the barred door, masking his true intentions.
context: He spoke in a jesting tone, but there was no jest in his eyes as he looked at me. I read suspicion there and annoyance, but no jest.

description: Mr. Rucastle is married to Mrs. Rucastle, and they reside together at Copper Beeches.
context: Mr. and Mrs. Rucastle are going on a visit, and will be away all the evening

description: Mr. Rucastle controls the mastiff, using it as a tool of intimidation and threat against Miss Hunter.
context: I knew that he was the only one in the household who had any influence with the savage creature... I'll throw you to the mastiff.

description: Toller possesses and carries the black linen bag, which is central to the mystery of the barred room.
context: I once saw him carrying a large black linen bag with him through the door.

description: The investigation of the forbidden room takes place within and around the dark room.
context: I have made my dark room up there... The centre door was closed... I suddenly heard the sound of steps within the room

description: The threat involving the mastiff is issued within the Copper Beeches household.
context: I'll throw you to the mastiff.

description: The execution of Mr. Holmes's plan is set to take place at the Copper Beeches estate.
context: We shall be at the Copper Beeches by seven o'clock, my friend and I. The Rucastles will be gone by that time

source_name: The Mystery of the Barred Room
description: The mystery of the barred room directly involves Miss Hunter as the central observer and investigator.
context: I was foolish enough to go into the empty wing... The centre door was closed, and across the outside of it had been fastened one of the broad bars of an iron bed

source_name: The Mystery of the Barred Room
description: The mystery of the barred room involves Mr. Rucastle as the keeper of the secret and the one who barricades the room.
context: I have made my dark room up there... He spoke in a jesting tone, but there was no jest in his eyes

Holmes deduces the true identity and imprisoned status of Miss Alice Rucastle.
context: I have no doubt that it is the daughter, Miss Alice Rucastle, if I remember right, who was said to have gone to America.

description: Holmes identifies the man in the road as a friend and possible fiancé of Miss Alice Rucastle.
context: The man in the road was undoubtedly some friend of hers-possibly her fianc´ e-and no doubt, as you wore the girl's dress and were so like her, he was convinced from your laughter, whenever he saw you, and afterwards from your gesture, that Miss Rucastle was perfectly happy

description: The man in the road is suspected to be Miss Alice Rucastle's fiancé.
context: The man in the road was undoubtedly some friend of hers-possibly her fianc´ e

description: Mr. Rucastle holds Miss Alice Rucastle captive in the chamber.
context: You have been brought there to personate someone, and the real person is imprisoned in this chamber.

description: The child's disposition manifests as abnormally cruel behavior.
context: This child's disposition is abnormally cruel, merely for cruelty's sake

description: The child's cruel nature bodes evil for the imprisoned girl.
context: This child's disposition is abnormally cruel, merely for cruelty's sake, and whether he derives this from his smiling father, as I should suspect, or from his mother, it bodes evil for the poor girl who is in their power.

description: Mr. Rucastle is suspected to be the father of the cruel child.
context: whether he derives this from his smiling father, as I should suspect, or from his mother

description: Watson collaborates with Holmes as his medical and investigative partner.
context: My dear Watson, you as a medical man are continually gaining light as to the tendencies of a child by the study of the parents.

description: Miss Hunter expresses confidence in Holmes's deductions and plans.
context: I am sure that you are right, Mr. Holmes,' cried our client. 'A thousand things come back to me which make me certain that you have hit it.

description: Miss Hunter seeks to bring help to the imprisoned Miss Alice Rucastle.
context: Oh, let us lose not an instant in bringing help to this poor creature.

description: Holmes travels to and investigates the Copper Beeches estate.
context: We were as good as our word, for it was just seven when we reached the Copper Beeches, having put up our trap at a wayside public-house.

description: Watson accompanies Holmes to the Copper Beeches estate.
context: We were as good as our word, for it was just seven when we reached the Copper Beeches, having put up our trap at a wayside public-house.

description: Mrs. Toller is confined to the cellar by Mr. Rucastle.
context: I'd have done so before now if I could ha' got out from the cellar.

description: Toller is found resting in the kitchen.
context: Her husband lies snoring on the kitchen rug.

description: Toller is the husband of Mrs. Toller.
context: Her husband lies snoring on the kitchen rug.

description: Toller possesses duplicate keys to the chamber.
context: Here are his keys, which are the duplicates of Mr. Rucastle's.

description: Mr. Rucastle is the original owner of the chamber keys.
context: Here are his keys, which are the duplicates of Mr. Rucastle's.

description: Holmes aids Miss Hunter in her plan to rescue the prisoner.
context: Now lead the way, and we shall soon see the end of this black business.

description: Holmes enters and investigates the chamber where the prisoner was held.
context: We passed up the stair, unlocked the door, followed on down a passage, and found ourselves in front of the barricade which Miss Hunter had described.

description: Holmes cuts the cord and removes the transverse bar to breach the barricade.
context: Holmes cut the cord and removed the transverse bar.

description: Holmes attempts to use the keys to unlock the chamber door.
context: Then he tried the various keys in the lock, but without success.

description: Holmes and Watson collaborate physically to force open the chamber door.
context: Now, Watson, put your shoulder to it, and we shall see whether we cannot make our way in.

description: Holmes leads the investigation and rescue effort at Copper Beeches.
context: We were as good as our word, for it was just seven when we reached the Copper Beeches

description: Watson assists Holmes in the rescue mission.
context: Now, Watson, put your shoulder to it, and we shall see whether we cannot make our way in.

description: Miss Hunter facilitates the rescue by providing keys and guidance.

properties.contribution: provides keys and leads the way

context: Here are his keys, which are the duplicates of Mr. Rucastle's.

description: Miss Alice Rucastle escapes from the chamber through the skylight.
context: The skylight above was open, and the prisoner gone.

description: Mr. Rucastle orchestrates or is responsible for the prisoner's escape.
context: this beauty has guessed Miss Hunter's intentions and has carried his victim off.

description: Mr. Rucastle uses a ladder to escape through the skylight.
context: here's the end of a long light ladder against the eaves. That is how he did it.

description: Mr. Rucastle owns the Copper Beeches estate.
context: the ladder was not there when the Rucastles went away.

description: Watson carries a revolver for protection during the investigation.
context: I have my revolver,' said I.

description: Mr. Rucastle carries a heavy stick when confronting Holmes.
context: a very fat and burly man, with a heavy stick in his hand.

description: Miss Hunter is terrified by Mr. Rucastle's appearance.
context: Miss Hunter screamed and shrunk against the wall at the sight of him

description: Holmes confronts and investigates Mr. Rucastle's villainy.
context: Sherlock Holmes sprang forward and confronted him. 'You villain!' said he, 'where's your daughter?'

description: Mr. Rucastle is the father of Miss Alice Rucastle.
context: 'You villain!' said he, 'where's your daughter?'

description: Mr. Rucastle uses the skylight to escape the chamber.
context: The fat man cast his eyes round, and then up at the open skylight.

description: Mr. Rucastle temporarily holds Holmes and his party captive.
context: You are in my power. I'll serve you! He turned and clattered down the stairs as hard as he could go.

description: Mr. Rucastle uses the hound as a guard to prevent communication with the prisoner.
context: The dog is let loose at night to prevent him from endeavouring to communicate with her.

description: The hound attacks Mr. Rucastle after being loosed.
context: There was the huge famished brute, its black muzzle buried in Rucastle's throat, while he writhed and screamed upon the ground.

description: Mr. Rucastle is the victim of the hound's attack.
context: There was the huge famished brute, its black muzzle buried in Rucastle's throat, while he writhed and screamed upon the ground.

description: Holmes rushes to the scene to investigate and assist after the attack.
context: Holmes and I rushed out and round the angle of the house, with Toller hurrying behind us.

description: Watson shoots the hound and rescues Mr. Rucastle.

properties.contribution: kills hound and provides medical aid

context: Running up, I blew its brains out, and it fell over with its keen white teeth still meeting in the great creases of his neck.

description: Toller follows Holmes and Watson to the scene of the attack.
context: Holmes and I rushed out and round the angle of the house, with Toller hurrying behind us.

description: Watson saves Mr. Rucastle from the hound and provides medical relief.
context: Running up, I blew its brains out, and it fell over with its keen white teeth still meeting in the great creases of his neck. With much labour we separated them and carried him, living but horribly mangled, into the house.

description: Holmes helps carry the mangled Mr. Rucastle into the house.
context: With much labour we separated them and carried him, living but horribly mangled, into the house.

description: Holmes enters the drawing-room where Mr. Rucastle is laid on the sofa.
context: We laid him upon the drawing-room sofa, and having dispatched the sobered Toller to bear the news to his wife, I did what I could to relieve his pain.

description: Mrs. Toller enters the drawing-room after being let out by Mr. Rucastle.
context: We were all assembled round him when the door opened, and a tall, gaunt woman entered the room. 'Mrs. Toller!' cried Miss Hunter.

description: Mrs. Toller possesses extensive knowledge of Mr. Rucastle's schemes.
context: It is clear that Mrs. Toller knows more about this matter than anyone else.

description: Mrs. Toller offers to help Miss Hunter but warns her plans are wasted.
context: Ah, miss, it is a pity you didn't let me know what you were planning, for I would have told you that your pains were wasted.

description: Holmes assesses Mrs. Toller's knowledge and motives with suspicion.

properties.quote: It is clear that Mrs. Toller knows more about this matter than anyone else.

context: 'Ha!' said Holmes, looking keenly at her. 'It is clear that Mrs. Toller knows more about this matter than anyone else.'

description: Mrs. Toller claims to have been Miss Alice's friend and intended to help her.
context: If there's police-court business over this, you'll remember that I was the one that stood your friend, and that I was Miss Alice's friend too.

description: Mr. Rucastle's actions are characterized by villainy.
context: There has been some villainy here,' said Holmes; 'this beauty has guessed Miss Hunter's intentions and has carried his victim off.

Mr. Rucastle is Alice's father, having remarried and assumed control over her life and finances.
context: from the time that her father married again

description: Mr. Rucastle is married to Mrs. Rucastle, who remains devoted to him despite his broken state.
context: kept alive solely through the care of his devoted wife

description: Alice met Mr. Fowler at a friend's house, initiating their relationship.
context: until after she met Mr. Fowler at a friend's house

description: Alice shares a deep romantic bond with Mr. Fowler, who remains steadfast despite her illness.
context: he stuck to her as true as man could be

description: Alice and Mr. Fowler are married by special license after their escape.
context: Mr. Fowler and Miss Rucastle were married, by special license, in Southampton the day after their flight

description: Mr. Fowler works with Miss Violet Hunter to secure his interests and escape.
context: having met you succeeded by certain arguments, metallic or otherwise, in convincing you that your interests were the same as his

description: Miss Violet Hunter cooperates with Mr. Fowler, recognizing their shared interests.
context: convincing you that your interests were the same as his

description: Miss Violet Hunter lives at the Sinister House after being brought down from London.
context: brought Miss Hunter down from London in order to get rid of the disagreeable persistence of Mr. Fowler

description: Miss Violet Hunter originally resided in London before being brought to the Rucastle house.
context: brought Miss Hunter down from London

description: Miss Violet Hunter works and resides in Walsall, where she runs a private school.
context: she is now the head of a private school at Walsall, where I believe that she has met with considerable success

description: Mr. Fowler resides on the Island of Mauritius after obtaining a government appointment.
context: he is now the holder of a government appointment in the island of Mauritius

description: Mr. Fowler was married in Southampton, marking a key location in his life.
context: Mr. Fowler and Miss Rucastle were married, by special license, in Southampton the day after their flight

description: Alice Rucastle lives at the Sinister House under the control of her father.
context: She was never happy at home, Miss Alice wasn't, from the time that her father married again

description: Mr. Rucastle resides at the Sinister House, which serves as the base for his operations.
context: Mr. Rucastle survived, but was always a broken man, kept alive solely through the care of his devoted wife. They still live with their old servants

description: Mrs. Rucastle resides at the Sinister House alongside her husband and servants.
context: They still live with their old servants, who probably know so much of Rucastle's past life

description: Holmes investigates Miss Violet Hunter's case to uncover the truth behind the Rucastle household.
context: I am sure we owe you an apology, Mrs. Toller,' said Holmes, 'for you have certainly cleared up everything which puzzled us

description: Holmes and Watson collaborate closely, with Watson assisting in the investigation and escort.
context: so I think, Watson, that we had best escort Miss Hunter back to Winchester

description: Watson works alongside Holmes, participating in the escort and investigation.
context: so I think, Watson, that we had best escort Miss Hunter back to Winchester

description: Miss Violet Hunter is escorted back to Winchester, indicating it as her destination.
context: we had best escort Miss Hunter back to Winchester

description: Holmes is escorted back to Winchester alongside Miss Hunter.
context: we had best escort Miss Hunter back to Winchester

description: Alice suffers from brain-fever as a direct result of her father's psychological pressure.

properties.contribution: suffered severe illness for six weeks

context: he kept on worrying her until she got brain-fever, and for six weeks was at death's door

description: Mr. Rucastle causes Alice's brain-fever through persistent psychological worry.
context: he kept on worrying her until she got brain-fever

description: Mr. Fowler organizes and executes the blockade of the house to pressure the occupants.
context: Mr. Fowler being a persevering man, as a good seaman should be, blockaded the house

description: Miss Violet Hunter participates in the blockade by cooperating with Mr. Fowler.
context: having met you succeeded by certain arguments, metallic or otherwise, in convincing you that your interests were the same as his

description: Mrs. Toller assists Mr. Fowler, providing him with resources and support.
context: Mr. Fowler was a very kind-spoken, free-handed gentleman,' said Mrs. Toller serenely

description: Mrs. Toller controls the ladder, keeping it ready for the escape plan.
context: a ladder should be ready at the moment when your master had gone out

description: Mr. Rucastle seeks to control The Paper to secure Alice's money regardless of her marital status.
context: He wanted her to sign a paper, so that whether she married or not, he could use her money

description: Alice Rucastle refuses to sign The Paper, resisting her father's financial control.
context: When she wouldn't do it, he kept on worrying her

description: Mr. Rucastle employs the imprisonment system to keep Alice isolated and controlled.
context: Mr. Rucastle then, I presume, took to this system of imprisonment?

description: The imprisonment system is carried out at the Sinister House.
context: Mr. Rucastle then, I presume, took to this system of imprisonment?

description: The blockade takes place at the Sinister House to pressure the occupants.
context: blockaded the house, and having met you succeeded by certain arguments

description: Mr. Fowler holds a government appointment, which secures his future on the Island of Mauritius.
context: he is now the holder of a government appointment in the island of Mauritius

description: Miss Violet Hunter heads a private school in Walsall, marking her successful post-case life.
context: she is now the head of a private school at Walsall, where I believe that she has met with considerable success

description: Alice possesses rights by will, which she initially keeps quiet about.
context: Miss Alice had rights of her own by will, but she was so quiet and patient, she was, that she never said a word about them

description: Mr. Rucastle seeks to control the rights by will to secure Alice's money.
context: left everything in Mr. Rucastle's hands. He knew he was safe with her

description: Holmes questions the legal standing (locus standi) of their continued presence at the house.
context: our locus standi now is rather a questionable one

description: Watson is involved in the questionable locus standi alongside Holmes.
context: our locus standi now is rather a questionable one

description: Miss Violet Hunter's cooperation and testimony help solve the mystery of the imprisonment system.
context: you have certainly cleared up everything which puzzled us

description: Mrs. Rucastle assists her husband by providing care and keeping him alive.
context: kept alive solely through the care of his devoted wife
